summary: Hotz reflects on returning to America after three months abroad, expressing deep pessimism about American society and culture. He argues that 'wokism' is evolved Protestantism and that American religious society will struggle with AI's revelation of human unspecialness. He positions Elon Musk as a kingmaker figure similar to Trump's role in the Republican party, suggesting Elon may now have complete power to choose election winners. Hotz advocates for a 'pro-growth' political framework centered around Musk and announces his own plans to leave California and potentially America entirely.
key_points: American social fabric has deteriorated, visible in the lack of pride and care among workers compared to places like Hong Kong
AI progress is the only issue that matters and will change society more than people can imagine - demonstrated by his 7th place Advent of Code ranking using AI
Elon Musk has become a political kingmaker who may have complete power to choose election winners, similar to Trump's role since 2016
Chinese religious tradition (Buddhism/Taoism with Confucian worldview) will adapt to AI better than Western religious frameworks
Western society faces a cultural crisis because 'the soul isn't real' and people's expectations exceed their abilities - AI will expose this further
money_quotes: America has had its social fabric torn to shreds.
Wokism is really just Protestantism evolved, it's not an aberration.
This is the only real issue that matters. It will change society more than you can possibly believe.
It's possible Elon now actually has the complete power to choose the winner.
Anti-Elon will not be a tenable political position, and this is a good thing.
The soul isn't real, and this will be a very hard pill for many westerners to swallow.
In rich Western society people's expectations exceed their abilities. AI will pummel this even harder.
The money is the map, not the territory! If you pervert a map you don't change the territory, you are just lost.
key_insight: AI's rapid advancement will expose the myth of human specialness, and societies with less individualistic religious frameworks will adapt better than the spiritually-invested West.
